coredata.dc:title: Density of states analysis of electrostatic confinement in gapped graphene
coredata.dc:creator: Bouhlal, Ahmed
Jellal, Ahmed
Bahlouli, Hocine
coredata.dc:description: We investigate the electrostatic confinement of charge carriers in a gapped graphene quantum dot in the presence of a magnetic flux. The circular quantum dot is defined by an electrostatic gate potential demarcated in a large intrinsic graphene circular ribbon which is then connected to a circular lead. Considering different regions composing our system, we explicitly determine the energy spectrum solutions in terms of Hankel functions using the scattering matrix approach. The asymptotic behavior of the Hankel functions for large arguments allowed us to calculate the density of states (DOS) and show that it has an oscillatory behavior with the appearance of some resonant peaks associated with the quantum dot bound states with definite angular momenta. It was also found that the energy gap can control the amplitude and width of these resonances and affect even their location as reflected in the density of states profile.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dot
Magnetic flux
Potential
Energy gap
Density of states

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Density of states and scattering matrices

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The generous support provided by the Saudi Center for Theoretical Physics (SCTP) is highly appreciated by Dr Jellal. AJ and HB acknowledge the support of King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals under research group project RG181001 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: ( c olor online) Gate-defined graphene quantum dot (gold color) surrounded by an intrinsic graphene circular sheet and coupled to a circular lead (gray color). . (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

( c olor online) Plotted is the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at incident energy ε
τ = 0 for ratio R/L = 0.2, different values of the energy gap Rδ and for the two valleys τ = ±1. The resonances are labeled according to their angular momentum m = ±1/2, …, ±9/2. The two uppers figures are for valley index τ = + 1 while the two lowers ones are for valley index τ = −1. Panels (a,c) have been plotted for Rδ = 0, 0.5, 1 while panels (b,d) have been plotted for Rδ = 2, 3, 4. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

( c olor online) Shown is the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at incident energy ε
τ = 0 for ratio R/L = 0.2 and different values of the energy gap Rδ = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. In panel (a) we have the valley index τ = 1 for the first resonance m = 1/2. In panel (b) we show the case τ = −1 for first resonance m = 1/2. In panel (c) we show the case τ = 1 for second resonance m = 3/2. In panel (d) we consider τ = −1 for the second resonance m = 3/2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

( c olor online) Plotted is the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at incident energy ε
τ = 0 for the first resonance m = 1/2 for different values of the energy gap and valley index τ and ratio R/L . The plot in panel (a) is for Rδ = 0. In panel (b) we have considered Rδ = 4 and valley index τ = 1. In panel (c) we considered Rδ = 4 and valley index τ = −1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

( c olor online) Shown is the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at incident energy ε
τ = 0 and magnetic flux φ = 1/2 for R/L = 0.2, different values of the energy gap Rδ and for two valley indices τ = ±1. Here the resonances are labeled according to their angular momentum μ = m + 1/2, with μ = ±1, …, ±4. In panels (a,c) we consider the valley index τ = + 1 and in panels (b,d) for the valley index τ = −1. In panels (a,c) we considered Rδ = 0, 0.5, 1 and in panels (b,d) we considered Rδ = 2, 3, 4. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

( c olor online) Plotted is the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at incident energy ε
τ = 0 and magnetic flux φ = 1/2 for R/L = 0.2 and different values of the energy gap Rδ = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Panel (a) has been plotted for valley index τ = 1 and μ = 1. Panel (b) is for first resonance μ = 1 and valley index τ = −1. Panel (c) has been plotted for second resonance μ = 2 and for valley index τ = 1. Panel (d) for second resonance μ = 2 and valley index τ = −1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

( c olor online) Shown is the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at incident energy ε
τ = 0 and first resonance μ = 1 for different values of the energy gap Rδ and ratio R/L . (a): Rδ = 0, (b): Rδ = 4 for τ = + 1, (c): Rδ = 4 for τ = −1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

( c olor online) Plotted is the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at incident energy ε
τ = 0 for R/L = 0.2. (a) and (b): Rδ = 0 for five values of magnetic flux φ = 0 (line red), 1/2 (green line), 3/2 (blue line), 5/2 (magenta line) and 7/2 (line purple). (c): Rδ = 4, τ = −1 (blue line) and τ = + 1 (red line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Density of states analysis of electrostatic confinement in gapped graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University, PO Box 20, 24000, El Jadida, Morocco
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaib Doukkali University, PO Box 20, 24000 El Jadida, Morocco

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Canadian Quantum Research Center, 204-3002 32 Ave, Vernon, BC, V1T 2L7, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Canadian Quantum Research Center, 204-3002 32 Ave Vernon, BC V1T 2L7, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals, Dhahran, 31261, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals, Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University, PO Box 20, 24000, El Jadida, Morocco.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigate the electrostatic confinement of charge carriers in a gapped graphene quantum dot in the presence of a magnetic flux. The circular quantum dot is defined by an electrostatic gate potential demarcated in a large intrinsic graphene circular ribbon which is then connected to a circular lead. Considering different regions composing our system, we explicitly determine the energy spectrum solutions in terms of Hankel functions using the scattering matrix approach. The asymptotic behavior of the Hankel functions for large arguments allowed us to calculate the density of states (DOS) and show that it has an oscillatory behavior with the appearance of some resonant peaks associated with the quantum dot bound states with definite angular momenta. It was also found that the energy gap can control the amplitude and width of these resonances and affect even their location as reflected in the density of states profile.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene 2D system has been subject to intense theoretical and experimental interest due to its unusual electronic properties [1]. The low-energy electronic properties of graphene are described in terms of massless Dirac electrons near two inequivalent “valleys” at momenta K and K’, called Dirac points. Close to Dirac points, the electrons can be effectively modeled by a massless Dirac equation, showing that electrons behave as chiral particles [2]. The characteristic feature of the massless Dirac electrons in graphene and their linear energy dispersion are at the origin of its unique electronic properties that could be of great importance in nanoelectronic applications [3,4]. The electronic band structure of graphene involves two nodal zero-gap points (K, K′), called Dirac points, in the first Brillouin zone at which the conduction and valence bands touch. This leads to a number of its unusual peculiar electronic properties such as its high electric conductivity [5,6]. Besides, electrons and holes in this material behave like ultra-relativistic particles. This led to an upsurge in theoretical investigations of the physical properties of this system, which became a testbed allowing us to simulate subtle and hardly achievable relativistic effects of high energy physics in our laboratories.

However, one of the most important drawbacks in potential future nanoelectronics application is the difficulty to confine electrons in graphene using the traditional approach based on electrostatic potentials. The general argument against the possibility to confine electrons electrostatically in graphene is due to Klein tunneling, which hindered the possibility to use this marvelous material in electronic switching devices that require a gate control over charge carriers [7]. Thus, pristine graphene quantum dots (GQDs) will allow electron to escape from any confining electrostatic potential and will not allow for quantum bound states in an electrostatically confined quantum dot. Since one can suppress Klein tunneling by introducing a band gap near the Dirac points, then a large amount of research efforts were directed toward engineering such a gap in the electronic energy spectrum of graphene by controlling its lateral size and shape and create a band gap that allows for charge confinement in GQDs in various ways. This feature along with the characteristic size of the quantum dot, usually in the size range of 1–10 nm, will enable us to control a wide range of applications including highly tunable physicochemical and fluorescence properties as well as other electrical and optoelectronic properties.

The recent advances in controlled manufacturing of high quality GQDs as well as its strictly two-dimensional nature have established graphene as an exceptional candidate for future nano electronic devices [6,8,9]. For this reason the experimental activity aimed at confining electrons in GQDs [10] had an upsurge in recent years. Theoretically, in the absence of a spectral gap, it has been shown that an electrostatically confined QD can only accommodate quasi-linked states [11]. At Dirac point, i.e. at energy E = 0, where the valence and conduction bands touch, electronic transport through QDs of certain shapes has also been considered [12]. In this particular situation, strong resonances in the two-terminal conductance have been predicted. However, in the presence of a spectral gap, real bound states have been obtained [13,14]. The physical methods used to open a gap in the energy spectrum of graphene are of vital importance for future potential applications [6]. On the other hand, the application of a magnetic flux in GQDs allows to control and strengthen the possibility of electrostatically confining fermions [15]. It was shown that the magnetic flux shifts the kinematic angular momentum to integer values, hence allowing confinement for states that cannot be confined by electrostatic gate potentials alone [12].

Based on our former work [16], we study the electrostatic confinement of electrons in a quantum dot of gapped graphene, surrounded by a circular sheet of intrinsic graphene, in the presence of the magnetic flux. We assume that the quantum dot edge smearing is much less than the Fermi wavelength of the electrons and much larger than the graphene lattice constant to ensure the validity of our continuum model [17]. Solving Dirac equation in each region and applying the continuity of our spinors at the boundary enables us to determine the solutions in each region of space and the corresponding energy spectrum. Subsequently, we use the asymptotic behavior of Hankel functions for large arguments to study approximately the density of states (DOS) as a function of magnetic flux φ, energy gap Δ and applied electrostatic potential V. We numerically compute the DOS under suitable selections of the physical parameters and investigate the different oscillatory behaviors and the resulting resonances as well as the dependence of the DOS peaks on the quantum momentum numbers and strength of the energy gap.

It is worth mentioning that, in general, the low energy effective theory of graphene results in two degenerate valleys that contribute equally to the transport properties. These valleys are located at two inequivalent points at the edge of the Brillouin zone called K and K′. This valley degeneracy is a consequence of the sublattice (chiral) and time-reversal symmetries of graphene. Breaking of the sublattice symmetry in graphene can be translated into a mass term in the Dirac-Weyl Hamiltonian. In our situation, we consider a sublattice dependent graphene substrate interaction which leads to the creation of a valley dependent energy gap (e.g. using a hexagonal boron-nitride (h-BN) substrate). Thus, our system breaks valley degeneracy and this effect should be reflected on the transport properties and density of states profile of the system.

The manuscript is organized as follows. In section 2, we set our theoretical model describing electrostatically confined Dirac fermions. The energy spectrum is given in each region of our system. In section 3, we introduce the scattering matrix formalism to determine the DOS in terms of various physical parameters. We compute the DOS and present our results, which reflect the effect of magnetic flux and energy gap on the resonant peaks in the DOS. In section 4, we further discuss different numerical results related to the density of the states and then summarize our findings in the final section.

We consider a quantum dot (QD) defined by a gate with finite-carrier density and surrounded by a circular sheet of pristine graphene, which is connected to a circular metallic contact lead as depicted in Fig. 1 . Taking the gate potential to be smooth on the scale of the lattice spacing, the electron dynamics in a circular electrostatically defined quantum dot in gapped graphene can then be described by the following Hamiltonian
(1)

i are Pauli matrices in the basis of the two sublattices of A and B atoms, the index τ = ±1 labels the valleys K and K′. Note that, the energy gap Δ(r) is due to graphene substrate interaction, which creates a valley dependent energy gap. The scalar potential barrier V(r) and Δ(r) are defined by

We choose the parameters V 0 and V

∞ → ∞. The chief reason for our choice of a piece-wise uniform potential is to enable us to proceed further with analytic calculations but our main results do not dependent critically on the details of this simplified model.

The cylindrical symmetry of our system dictates the usage of polar coordinates (r, θ) as being the most suitable coordinates, we also introduce the vector potential that generates a solenoid type of magnetic flux

The presence of half quantum magnetic flux φ = 1/2 modifies the eigenspinors (13), because the kinematic angular momentum will then be replaced by the canonical one, i.e. J
z,kin = J

In next section, we will show how the above results can be used to analyze the density of states associated with our quantum dot. This will be done by distinguishing the two situations, in the absence and presence of the magnetic flux.

To reach a better understanding of the main spectral features of our system, let us investigate the effect of the valley dependent energy gap on the density of states (DOS). For this purpose, we introduce the local DOS ν(r, ε) that is given in terms of the scattering matrix S
(
ϵ
)
[18–20].

To calculate ν dot at zero energy (ε = 0) as a function of the quantum dot parameters, it suffices to solve the Dirac equation associated with the Hamiltonian (1), for small but finite energy ε
τ , and determine the scattering matrix S. This will be done by considering the zero and nonzero magnetic flux cases separately.

In the present case and for r > L, the eigenspinors can be written as a linear combination of the two solutions of (13)

We consider the limit of a highly doped lead k

where ln
γ
E
= 0.577 ⋯
is Euler's constant. For negative integer, m < 0, we have the relation J −m = (−1) m
J
m and Y −m = (−1) m
Y
m . For small energy ε, we can develop the scattering matrix as a function of κ in the region R < r < L and choose χ 1,2(r) ∝ J
n (kr) regular at r = 0. We then find

We now use (20) to calculate DOS ν dot at zero energy for both cases m ≠

. Our calculation shows that

The first term in (32) represents the integral of the local DOS inside the QD region (r < R), while the second one represents the local DOS integral in the undoped layer that separates QD and the metallic contact [17]. On the other hand, for zero energy using the continuity of the eigenspinors (14) and (8) at r = R, gives the resonance condition
(33)

, (τ 2 = 1) and the dimensionless resonance width is given by

.
Thus for a disk-shaped dot of radius R we expect narrower resonances for higher values of angular momenta in the limit of very small dot size R compared to the contact size L.

We now investigate the density of states for a gapped graphene quantum dot in the presence of a magnetic flux, such that eigenspinors are those given by (15) and (18) taking into consideration the kinematic angular momentum μ. The states with zero kinematic angular momentum need to be discussed separately in the presence and absence of magnetic flux. We first discuss the states with μ ≠ 0, where the magnetic field only leads to slight modifications. Effectively, one finds that the results of (23) and (29) remain valid, as far as the half-integer index m is replaced by the integer index μ. For μ ≠ 0, the calculation of bound states proceeds in the same way as in the absence of flux and we find that the resonance condition is given by
(38)
J
| μ |
− 1 / 2
(
κ
0
′
R
)
= 0 .
We conclude that, if the quantum dot and the surrounding undoped graphene layer are made in contact with a lead, the width Γ of the resonances is given by
(39)
Γ = 2
(
R / L
)
2 μ
.
For the case μ = 0, regularity of the wave function at the origin is not sufficient to determine the scattering matrix S 0(ε). Taking a flux line of extended diameter, we find out that the wave function has to vanish at the origin [21]. The calculation of the scattering matrix S 0(ε) in this case is straightforward and leads to the following result
(40)
S
0
=
e
− 2 i
(
κ
∞
−
κ
0
τ
)
R
e
− 2 i
(
κ
∞
− κ
)
(
L − R
)
where κ,
κ
0
τ
and κ
∞ are given in (12) as functions of the gate voltage V and energy gap Δ. Note that by requiring Δ = 0, we recover the DOS derived in Ref. [17]. The analytical results obtained so far will be numerically analyzed to investigate how the electrostatic confinement in gapped graphene quantum dot is reflected in the density of states.

We study the influence of the introduced energy gap and its valley dependence as reflected through the parameter τ in presence of a magnetic flux φ = 1/2 at an incident energy ε
τ = 0 on the bound states of an electrostatically confined graphene quantum dot of radius R and contact size L. Indeed, because the parameter of our theory is
κ
0
τ
=
|
V
0
2
−
δ
2
|
, then we choose to numerically analyze the DOS versus the gate voltage V 0 R for a suitable selection of the physical parameters. More precisely, we consider the following particular values of the ratio R/L=(0.05, 0.07, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2) energy gap Rδ = (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), angular quantum numbers m = (1/2, 3/2⋯) for zero flux, μ = (1, 2⋯) for nonzero flux for the two valley τ = ±1.

Let us first discuss the DOS in the absence of magnetic flux, the DOS for a circular quantum dot as function of the gate voltage V 0 R at ε
τ = 0 for R/L = 0.2 and different values of the energy gap Rδ and for two valley indices τ = ±1, is shown in Fig. 2 . We observe that the DOS exhibits an oscillatory behavior with the appearance of resonance peaks, which are labeled according to their angular quantum momentum m. This behavior shows that when the energy gap Rδ increases, the amplitude of DOS decreases with a shift of its resonances to the right for τ = + 1, see panels 2(a,c). On the other hand for τ = −1, the amplitude and width increase while the resonance peaks move towards the left, see panels 2(b,d), as we increase the gap Rδ. Note that for Rδ = 0, the position of resonances as well as width and amplitude are in full agreement with the results obtained in the literature [17,22,23]. It is clearly seen that for higher values of angular momentum m, the resonance widths decrease so that they appear as sharp peaks.

The plot of the DOS shows clearly the first resonance m = 1/2 and second resonance m = 3/2 for different values of Rδ = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and for the two valley indices τ = ±1. We deduce that the resonance characteristics depend on both the valley index τ and magnitude of the energy gap δ. Indeed, from panels 3(a,c) and for τ = + 1, the resonance positions shift so that the amplitude and width of the resonance decrease if Rδ increases. Whereas from panels 3(b,d) and for τ = −1, there appear sharp peaks at the location which corresponds to the chosen values of Rδ. We also notice that the first and second resonances are duplicated when V 0 R ≥ Rδ = 3, panels 3(a,c), and V 0 R ≥ Rδ = 4, panels 3 (b,d). We observe that the DOS exhibits an oscillatory behavior with decreased amplitude when V 0 R increases for δ ≥ 0 [17] and an increase in the oscillation amplitude for δ ≤ 0. Moreover, the width of the second resonance in the DOS, panels 3(c,d), is very narrow as compared to first resonance, panels 3(a,b).

To show how the first resonance m = 1/2 behaves when we change the energy gap Rδ and its valley index τ and increase the contact size L for a fixed radius R, we present the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R in Fig. 4
, with (a): Rδ = 0, (b) and (c): Rδ = 4 such as (b) for τ = + 1 and (c) for τ = −1. It is clearly seen that when R/L is very small, the DOS saturates (maximum), which could be explained by invoking the weak coupling between the QD and the metallic contacts in this limit [17]. Now by comparing panels 4(a,b,c), we notice that the amplitudes of resonances increase as we decrease the dot size relative to the contact size R/L when τ = −1.

In Fig. 5 , we show the DOS as a function of the gate voltage V 0 R for R/L = 0.2 for different values of Rδ and valley index τ where the resonances are labeled according to their angular momentum μ = m + 1/2. We observe that the amplitude of DOS decreases as we increase Rδ with a shift to the right for τ = + 1, panels 5(a,c), and a shift to left for τ = −1, panels 5(b,d). The presence of the magnetic flux causes the elimination of the resonance corresponding to m = −1/2, that is, no normalizable bound state exists for this value of m.

In Fig. 6 , we show the DOS as function of the gate voltage V 0 R in the presence of half quantum magnetic flux φ = 1/2 at incident energy ε
τ = 0 for R/L = 0.2 and different values of the energy gap Rδ and for the two valleys τ. Panel 6(a) corresponds to τ = + 1 and δR = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 with μ = 1 (first resonance), which shows that the DOS exhibits oscillations whose amplitudes decrease by increasing the energy gap δR. In addition, there is a duplication of the number of peaks as compared to the situation with δR = 4. In panel 6(b) we consider the valley index τ = −1 and choose the values Rδ = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 with μ = 1 (first resonance), we notice the same behavior as that in panel 6(a) except that when the value of Rδ increases the amplitude of DOS increases. For a valley index τ = + 1, and values Rδ = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and μ = 2 (second resonance), panel 6(c) shows the appearance of peaks for each value of Rδ. The height of the peaks decreases when Rδ increases, we also notice a doubling of number of resonances as compared to the situation with Rδ = 5. Now for the valley index τ = −1, values Rδ = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, with μ = 2 (second resonance), panel 6(d) shows the same behavior as that in panel 6(c) except that when the value of Rδ increases the oscillation amplitudes increase. Note that if we compare Fig. 3 (absence of magnetic flux) and Fig. 6 (presence of magnetic flux), we conclude that the energy gap in the presence of magnetic flux increases the heights and decreases the widths of the oscillation resonances.

In Fig. 7 , we show how the first resonance μ = 1 behaves when we change the energy gap Rδ for the two valley indices τ at incident energy ε
τ = 0 and magnetic flux φ = 1/2. Indeed, the plot shows that the resonance width increases, while the height decreases when the contact size L increases for a fixed value of R. From panels 7(b,c), we notice that the introduction of Rδ in the presence of magnetic flux allows to amplify the resonance height by a factor of 10 as compared to panels 4(b,c) for m = 1/2 (zero magnetic flux).

We now analyze the DOS for a graphene quantum dot in the presence of large magnetic flux φ by fixing the quantum momentum m = 1/2 and varying φ in Fig. 8 where its resonance peaks correspond to the different values of μ = 1/2, 1, 2, 3, 4. Panels 8(a,b) show that the DOS diverges as long as φ becomes very large because the DOS passes from 5 for φ = 0 to a very large value 100,000 for φ = 7/2. Now to analyze the situation in more detail, let us zoom in the first and second resonances and study its dependence on R/L as shown in panel 8(a). We extract the height and width of the resonance by adjustment to a Lorentzian, we observe that the first two resonance peaks are wide compared to the other peaks. We also zoom in the resonance peak corresponds to the value μ = 3 as indicated in panel 8(b), we notice that the DOS is doubled by changing φ from 5/2 to 7/2. Panel 8(c) shows the resonance peaks for μ = 4 and energy gap Rδ = 4 for the two valleys τ = ±1. As a consequence, for τ = −1 the DOS diverges and it is doubled.

Thus in the case of a large magnetic flux there should be a diverging density of states at ε
τ = 0 [24].

Comparing our results to the DOS analysis reported in Ref. [17], we have some comments in order. Indeed, we noticed that the inclusion of an energy gap δ in a graphene quantum dot of radius R in presence of a magnetic flux φ = 1/2 changes the resonance profiles in the DOS. More precisely, we noticed that the amplitudes and widths of the resonances decrease for the case of the valley index τ = + 1, but they increase otherwise and the positions of the resonances are shifted. In addition, we observe the appearance of resonance peaks when Rδ is greater than critical value, which is well defined for each selection of the physical parameters considered. In summary, the energy gap δ amplifies the DOS in the presence of a magnetic flux and therefore we concluded that it can be used as a tunable parameter to control the transport properties of our system. Of course the DOS results obtained in Ref. [17] have been recovered by switching off the energy gap δ.

We have studied the confinement of charge carriers in an electrostatically gate delimited graphene quantum dot surrounded by a circular sheet of pristine graphene and connected to a single circular metallic contact in the presence of both an energy gap and a magnetic flux. We have solved the corresponding stationary Dirac equation and obtained analytically the energy spectrum solutions in the three regions composing our system. Due to cylindrical symmetry these solutions were expressed in terms of Hankel functions. We then derived an approximate formula for the density of states (DOS) as a function of magnetic flux, energy gap and the applied electrostatic potential. We have found the resonance condition at zero energy under suitable boundary conditions.

We have also shown that the DOS exhibits an oscillatory behavior in addition to the appearance of resonances at definite values of the angular quantum number m. The amplitude of the DOS oscillation resonances was found to decrease and shift to the right when δ increases for valley index τ = + 1 while they shift to the left when τ = −1. It was also observed that for very large values of the angular momentum m, the resonance width decreases and sharp peaks took over both in the presence and absence of magnetic flux. We have also shown that the presence of a magnetic flux eliminates the resonances which correspond to m = −1/2 while the resonances corresponding to m ≠ − 1/2 becomes sharper with amplification of their amplitudes. we conclude that in the presence of a larger magnetic field and in the presence of a gap energy Δ with τ = −1 that the density of states is very diverge at zero energy ε
τ = 0.

Finally we would like point out that whenever an external applied field couples differently to the carriers in the two valleys, then the valley degeneracy is lifted and interesting physical observation might be realized. In our present electrostatically delimited quantum dot boron-nitride (h-BN) substrate breaks the sublattice symmetry and induces an energy gap in the spectrum that couples differently to both valleys. We can also consider adding a uniform magnetic field that breaks the time reversal symmetry and hence lifts the valley degeneracy. This study will allow us to carry out a comparison with the graphene mass-profile quantum dot investigated recently [24]. We look forward to pursue these efforts in the near future.
All authors are contributed to the design and implementation of the research, to the analysis of results and to the writing of manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The generous support provided by the Saudi Center for Theoretical Physics (SCTP) is highly appreciated by Dr Jellal. AJ and HB acknowledge the support of King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals under research group project RG181001.

ce:section.ce:label: A Appendix
ce:section.ce:section-title: Density of states and scattering matrices
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, the scattering matrix elements S
m,n (ε, U(x)) is a function of the incident energy ε of the carriers and potential U(x). To linear order in a perturbation, δU(x), the density response and current response of the scattering problem can be expressed with the help of the local density of state (DOS) as function of position x and ε [20]
(A.1)
d
ν
m , n
(
x , ε
)
d ε

1

(
x
)
−
S
m , n
δ
S
m , n
†
δ U
(
x
)
and then the total density of states is given by
(A.2)
d ν
(
x , ε
)
d ε
=
∑
m , n
d
ν
m , n
d ε
.
Let us start from the fact that the 2N
p × 2N
p scattering matrix takes the form
(A.3)
S
(
ε
)
=
s
+ +

− +

− −
and is unitary
(A.4)
S
†

ε
)
S
(
ε
)
= I
Now by differentiating (A.4) with respect to energy and taking the trace over 2N
p propagating channels, we obtain
(A.5)

.

As application to our case, we first notice that the scattering states can be defined in the ring-shaped ideal contact. The local DOS ν(r, ε) at a particular position r is determined from the derivative of the scattering matrix S with respect to the potentialV(r)
(A.6)
ν
(
r , ε
)
= −
1

δ V
(
r
)
Then, the total density of states of the dot is then obtained by integrating over the region r < L
(A.7)

)

∫
